serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Velvet Truffle Spaghetti is approximately 350 g, comprising a full plate of cream-sauced spaghetti with truffle oil and parmesan.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with cream sauce, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; adapted with truffle oil and parmesan enrichment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Velvet Truffle Spaghetti exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked spaghetti (FDC ID 168936) combined with heavy cream sauce and parmesan cheese proportions typical of a restaurant truffle cream pasta. Truffle oil contributes negligible macronutrient change at typical usage levels (1–2 tsp per serving). Energy and macros are consistent with published values for creamy pasta dishes in USDA SR Legacy. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Spaghetti, white, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to cream-sauced truffle spaghetti
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of approximately 44–46 for white spaghetti, boiled al dente (mean ~45). Cream sauce and truffle oil additions do not significantly raise GI; fat and protein in the sauce may modestly lower the effective GI. GI of 45 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. GL per serving = (45 / 100) × (25.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.5 serving multiplier for 350 g) = (45/100) × 89.25 = 40.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1085 mg per full serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, approaching 47% of the 2300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~40 per serving) and refined carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–180 g) to limit glycemic load, and pair the dish with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt postprandial glucose response; the low GI of al dente spaghetti (~45) is partially offset by the large carbohydrate quantity in a full restaurant serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from heavy cream and parmesan (~5.8 g per 100 g; ~20 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, as a full serving delivers approximately 20 g of saturated fat, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 20 g for adults on a heart-healthy diet; requesting a lighter cream sauce or smaller portion is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Velvet Truffle Spaghetti is a premium Western-style pasta dish served at EC Coffee Bar in Singapore, featuring al dente spaghetti coated in a rich, velvety cream sauce infused with truffle oil and finished with shaved or minced black truffle and grated parmesan cheese. The dish is characterised by its luxurious, silky texture and the intensely earthy, umami-forward aroma of truffle, balanced by the mild sweetness of heavy cream and the sharp saltiness of aged parmesan. It is typically prepared by boiling spaghetti to al dente, then sautéing garlic in butter and olive oil, adding heavy cream, reducing to a sauce consistency, and finishing with truffle oil and parmesan. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~220 kcal per 100 g) with moderate protein (~6.8 g), significant fat (~10.5 g, predominantly saturated from dairy), and moderate refined carbohydrate (~25.5 g). The glycemic index is low (~45) due to the al dente pasta preparation, but the glycemic load per full serving is substantial (~40). Sodium content is elevated (~310 mg per 100 g), making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable. It is positioned as a premium café-restaurant offering in Singapore's Western-fusion dining scene, priced at SGD 31.80 per serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in truffle cream pasta?
What is the GI of truffle pasta?
How much sodium in truffle cream pasta?
What does truffle spaghetti taste like?
Where can I get Velvet Truffle Spaghetti in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Velvet Truffle Spaghetti exists in any approved primary regional or global nutrient database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked spaghetti and cream sauce components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti boiled al dente. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-based estimation is methodologically sound and consistent with published values for similar cream pasta dishes.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for this food category and is not reliably present in pasta or cream sauce at measurable levels in the cited source. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite used for this estimation. All other -1 values have been replaced with estimated numeric values where defensible component data was available. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (white spaghetti, boiled al dente); gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. GL per serving calculated as (45/100) × (25.5 × 3.5) = 40.2. The dish is served exclusively at EC Coffee Bar, Carpenter Street, Singapore, as of the field survey data seed record date. Price SGD 31.80. The cuisine tag 'Malaysian' in the input record likely reflects the restaurant's broader cuisine classification on field survey rather than the dish's culinary origin; the dish is Western-fusion in character and is classified accordingly. Halal status is assumed based on typical ingredients; consumers should verify with the restaurant directly.
